title: When forgetting is a moral act
summary: Argue that there are circumstances in which the deliberate refusal to remember — or the conscious permission to forget — is ethically required rather than morally negligent.
topic: The ethical case for forgetting: when memory becomes an obstacle to justice or repair
prompt: Write a 600–800-word argumentative essay arguing that forgetting — under certain conditions — is not a failure of memory or moral duty but an active ethical choice. Argue that the demand that people remember everything relevant to a grievance can be a form of cruelty, and that some forms of personal or social repair require the deliberate loosening of what memory insists on retaining. Use inversion at least once, a cleft sentence, and a 'granted… even so…' concession. Avoid turning this into an essay about political amnesty or historic crimes. Instead, treat forgetting as a question of personal ethics and relational life. 5–7 paragraphs.
hintStarters: Only rarely is forgetting treated as a deliberate ethical act rather than a passive failure of…
What the demand to remember everything refuses to acknowledge is the cost that sustained grievance imposes on…
Granted, there are wrongs that must not be forgotten — that forgetting would compound the original harm. Even so,…

hintVocab.phrase: to make a wrong permanently available
hintVocab.meaningVi: làm cho sai lầm luôn sẵn sàng được nhớ đến
tính cân xứng của phản ứng
ký ức như vũ khí đạo đức
sự vô tâm đầy từ bi
nghĩa vụ tiến về phía trước

hintVocab.phrase: to recalibrate what a wrong demands
hintVocab.meaningVi: tái cân bằng những gì một sai lầm đòi hỏi

sampleText:
Memory is not simply a record of what has happened; it is an argument about what matters. To remember something is to grant it continued relevance — to make it available as evidence in ongoing deliberations about what a person or a relationship owes. This is precisely why the question of what to forget is a moral question, not merely a psychological one. The decision to hold onto an injury, to rehearse it, to allow it to frame subsequent interactions, is an active choice, even when it does not feel like one. And so, under certain conditions, is its opposite.

Only rarely is forgetting treated as a deliberate ethical act rather than a passive failure of attention or a cowardly evasion of accountability. The dominant picture of moral memory is one in which remembering is virtuous and forgetting is suspect — a form of letting wrongdoers off the hook or abandoning one's obligations to those who were harmed. This picture captures something real. There are wrongs for which sustained memory is a form of witness, a refusal to let those who caused harm benefit from the passage of time. Not every form of letting go is a form of moving forward.

But the dominant picture obscures something equally real: that sustained grievance has costs, and that those costs are not always borne only by the person who caused the harm. When memory of a wrong becomes the primary lens through which a relationship is conducted, it can trap both parties in a script from which neither can deviate without seeming to excuse or to capitulate. The person who holds the grievance is held in place by it; the person against whom it is held cannot establish a present that is not already defined by the past. What begins as an appropriate response to harm can harden into a continuing present-tense harm of its own.

Granted, there are wrongs that must not be forgotten — that forgetting would compound the original harm by erasing what is owed. Even so, not every wrong falls into this category, and the difficulty is that the moral compulsion to remember can operate independently of whether remembering is actually serving any purpose beyond the maintenance of the grievance itself. When a wrong has been acknowledged, when accountability has been established, when the conditions for repair have been met, the continued rehearsal of the injury is no longer doing the moral work it once did; it is doing different work — the work of punishment, or of protection, or sometimes of identity. These may or may not be legitimate aims, but they deserve to be identified as such.

It is the conflation of memory with moral seriousness, not the act of forgetting itself, that most distorts this discussion. A person who decides to loosen memory's grip on a past injury is not declaring that the injury did not matter. They may be recognising that the purpose memory was originally serving has been served, and that continuing to serve it now incurs costs that are no longer proportionate to the end. This is not amnesia as evasion; it is recalibration — the difficult act of asking what a wrong still demands and finding, honestly, that the answer is less than it once was.

There is also a relational dimension that the language of moral obligation sometimes crowds out. Two people who share a history of harm — between them, not just inflicted by one upon the other — may find that repair requires both of them to agree, tacitly or explicitly, to hold certain things more lightly. This is not a betrayal of the past; it is a decision about what the future of the relationship requires. The ethics of forgetting is not simply the ethics of being wronged; it is also the ethics of what one is willing to offer in the reconstruction of a relationship that both parties want to sustain.

None of this grants impunity. Forgetting, as a moral act, requires that the wrong has registered — that accountability, in some form, has been established. What it refuses is the idea that memory is always and everywhere a virtue, that there is no such thing as grievance held past its proper season, that the ethics of the past is always more important than the ethics of the present. These are not axioms. They are assumptions that deserve the scrutiny they rarely receive.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Inversion after 'Only rarely' to challenge the dominant moral picture
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: Câu 'Only rarely is forgetting treated as a deliberate ethical act rather than a passive failure of attention' đảo trợ động từ 'is' trước chủ ngữ 'forgetting' sau 'Only rarely'. Vì động từ là 'treat' ở dạng bị động ('is treated'), trợ từ đảo là 'is'. Cấu trúc này phê phán giả định phổ biến mà không bác bỏ nó hoàn toàn.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'Only rarely is forgetting treated as a deliberate ethical act rather than a passive failure of attention' inverts the passive auxiliary 'is' before the subject 'forgetting' after 'Only rarely'. Because the verb is in the passive ('is treated'), the inverted element is the passive auxiliary. The inversion challenges a dominant assumption without dismissing it — a characteristically C1 move.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Cleft to locate the essay's key diagnostic error
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: Câu 'It is the conflation of memory with moral seriousness, not the act of forgetting itself, that most distorts this discussion' dùng cleft để chỉ ra rằng vấn đề không phải là quên lãng — mà là nhầm lẫn ký ức với đức hạnh đạo đức. Cấu trúc này là chiến lược: nó bảo vệ quên lãng bằng cách đặt trách nhiệm đúng chỗ.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'It is the conflation of memory with moral seriousness, not the act of forgetting itself, that most distorts this discussion' uses the cleft to identify the real problem: not forgetting, but the equation of memory with virtue. This is the essay's central diagnostic move — freeing forgetting from suspicion by locating the distortion elsewhere.

sampleTranslationVi:
Ký ức không chỉ đơn giản là hồ sơ về những gì đã xảy ra; đó là một lập luận về những gì quan trọng. Nhớ đến điều gì đó là trao cho nó sự liên quan liên tục. Đây chính xác là lý do tại sao câu hỏi về điều gì nên quên là câu hỏi đạo đức, không chỉ là câu hỏi tâm lý.

Hiếm khi sự quên lãng được coi là hành động đạo đức có chủ ý hơn là sự thất bại thụ động của sự chú ý. Bức tranh thống trị về ký ức đạo đức là bức tranh trong đó nhớ là đức hạnh và quên là đáng ngờ. Bức tranh này nắm bắt điều gì đó thực. Không phải mọi hình thức buông bỏ đều là hình thức tiến về phía trước.

Nhưng bức tranh thống trị che khuất điều gì đó cũng thực: rằng nỗi oán hận dai dẳng có những cái giá phải trả. Khi ký ức về một sai lầm trở thành ống kính chính qua đó mối quan hệ được thực hiện, nó có thể bẫy cả hai bên trong một kịch bản mà từ đó không ai có thể lệch khỏi mà không có vẻ bào chữa hoặc đầu hàng.

Cứ cho là, có những sai lầm không được phép quên lãng. Dù vậy, không phải mọi sai lầm đều thuộc loại này, và khi một sai lầm đã được thừa nhận, khi trách nhiệm đã được thiết lập, việc tiếp tục lặp đi lặp lại vết thương không còn thực hiện công việc đạo đức mà nó từng làm.

Chính sự pha trộn ký ức với sự nghiêm túc đạo đức, không phải hành động quên lãng bản thân, mới làm méo mó cuộc thảo luận này nhất. Một người quyết định nới lỏng sự kìm giữ của ký ức đối với một vết thương trong quá khứ không tuyên bố rằng vết thương đó không quan trọng. Đây không phải là quên lãng như sự né tránh; đó là tái cân bằng.

Cũng có chiều kích quan hệ mà ngôn ngữ nghĩa vụ đạo đức đôi khi lấn át. Hai người chia sẻ lịch sử về tổn hại có thể thấy rằng sửa chữa đòi hỏi cả hai đồng ý nắm giữ một số điều nhẹ nhàng hơn.

Quên lãng, như một hành động đạo đức, đòi hỏi rằng sai lầm đã được ghi nhận. Những gì nó từ chối là ý tưởng rằng ký ức luôn và ở mọi nơi là đức hạnh, rằng không có thứ gọi là nỗi oán hận được giữ quá mùa đúng của nó. Đây không phải là tiên đề. Đây là những giả định xứng đáng được xem xét kỹ lưỡng mà chúng hiếm khi nhận được.

mcQuestions.prompt: How does the essay define memory in its opening sentence?
options: As a neutral record of past events
As a faculty that deteriorates over time and requires cultivation
As an argument about what matters — granting continued relevance to what is remembered
As a social practice regulated by cultural norms
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay opens: 'Memory is not simply a record of what has happened; it is an argument about what matters. To remember something is to grant it continued relevance.'

mcQuestions.prompt: Which applies inversion correctly after 'Only rarely'?
options: Only rarely forgetting is treated as a deliberate ethical act.
Forgetting is only rarely treated as deliberate and ethical.
Only rarely, one would argue, does forgetting constitute an ethical act.
Only rarely is forgetting treated as a deliberate ethical act rather than a passive failure of attention.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Only rarely is forgetting treated…' inverts the passive auxiliary 'is' before the subject 'forgetting' — the standard inversion after a negative adverb fronting in a passive construction.
mcQuestions.hint: The verb is in passive voice ('is treated'), so the inverted auxiliary is 'is'.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'sustained memory is a form of witness' mean?
options: Continuing to remember a wrong can be an act of moral witness — refusing to let wrongdoers benefit from the passage of time
Reliable witnesses have strong long-term memories
Witnesses must sustain their account of events consistently in court
Memory is most sustained when shared between multiple witnesses
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay says 'there are wrongs for which sustained memory is a form of witness, a refusal to let those who caused harm benefit from the passage of time' — memory as active moral stance.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'a continuing present-tense harm of its own' describe?
options: A harm that occurs in the present, unrelated to past wrongs
The damage inflicted in the present by sustained rehearsal of a past grievance — both parties trapped in a script
The physical harm caused by a chronic medical condition
A current injustice that compounds a historical one
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay argues that when sustained grievance becomes the primary lens of a relationship, 'What begins as an appropriate response to harm can harden into a continuing present-tense harm of its own' — the grievance itself becomes an ongoing injury.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does the 'Granted… Even so…' structure concede and counter?
options: Grants that forgetting is psychologically difficult; counters that it is ethically required
Grants that memory has costs; counters that those costs are worth bearing
Grants that some wrongs must not be forgotten; counters that not every wrong falls into this category
Grants that forgetting is sometimes moral; counters that it requires legal amnesty
mcQuestions.explanation: The concession is that 'there are wrongs that must not be forgotten — that forgetting would compound the original harm.' The counter ('Even so') is that not every wrong is of this kind.
mcQuestions.hint: Look at the fourth paragraph carefully.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which uses a cleft to identify the real distortion in debates about forgetting?
options: It is the act of forgetting itself, not its conflation with evasion, that distorts this discussion.
What most distorts discussions of forgetting is the act itself rather than the assumptions surrounding it.
Forgetting and moral seriousness are both implicated equally in distorting this discussion.
It is the conflation of memory with moral seriousness, not the act of forgetting itself, that most distorts this discussion.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'It is the conflation of memory with moral seriousness, not the act of forgetting itself, that most distorts this discussion' correctly uses the cleft to locate the distortion in the assumption, not the act.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the 'It is … not … that …' structure in the fifth paragraph.

options: The use of forgetting to escape accountability for wrongs that have not been acknowledged
A medical condition caused by avoiding traumatic memories
A narrative technique in which a protagonist feigns memory loss
The tendency to forget inconvenient facts in political debates
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay uses 'amnesia as evasion' to describe the wrong kind of forgetting — where it serves to escape accountability rather than to release a grievance after accountability has been established.
mcQuestions.hint: Look at the fifth paragraph, where the phrase is contrasted with 'recalibration'.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'recalibration' mean in the essay's context?
options: Updating memory with new information as circumstances change
The act of honestly asking what a wrong still demands and finding the answer is less than it once was
Adjusting one's moral standards to fit changed circumstances
Reaching a new emotional equilibrium after a period of grief
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay defines recalibration as 'the difficult act of asking what a wrong still demands and finding, honestly, that the answer is less than it once was' — distinguishing it from evasion by the requirement of honesty.
mcQuestions.hint: Look at the end of the fifth paragraph.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'to rehearse an injury' mean?
options: To prepare legal evidence of harm for a court hearing
To practise recounting a trauma with a therapist
To repeatedly revisit and re-enact a past wrong in one's thoughts or interactions
To simulate an injury in a medical training context
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay uses 'rehearse' metaphorically — to rehearse an injury is to repeatedly return to it, keeping it active and present in one's thinking and relationships.
mcQuestions.hint: Think about what 'rehearsal' means in a theatrical context, then apply it to memory.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does the essay say about the relationship between forgetting and impunity?
options: Forgetting always grants impunity to those who caused harm
Impunity is an inevitable consequence of any act of forgetting
Impunity and forgetting are unrelated concepts
Forgetting, as a moral act, requires that the wrong has registered — it does not grant impunity
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay's final paragraph states: 'Forgetting, as a moral act, requires that the wrong has registered — that accountability, in some form, has been established.' Forgetting is conditional, not a blanket absolution.
mcQuestions.hint: Look at the opening of the final paragraph.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'grievance held past its proper season' mean?
options: A grievance that is maintained beyond the point at which it continues to serve a moral or practical purpose
A grievance that has been held for so long that its legal validity has expired
A complaint lodged too late to receive a formal institutional response
A wrong that occurred so long ago that it cannot be properly adjudicated
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay uses 'past its proper season' to suggest that grievances, like seasons, have appropriate durations — maintaining them past that point does not serve any legitimate purpose.
mcQuestions.hint: Think about what 'proper season' implies about timing and appropriateness.

mcQuestions.prompt: What relational condition does the essay say can make forgetting ethically appropriate?
options: When both parties to the harm have died
When both parties want to sustain the relationship and repair requires holding certain things more lightly
When a therapist has recommended it as part of a healing process
When the harm occurred more than ten years ago
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay argues: 'Two people who share a history of harm…may find that repair requires both of them to agree, tacitly or explicitly, to hold certain things more lightly.' The relational condition is mutual desire to sustain the relationship.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'proportionality of response' mean in the context of memory?
options: The idea that one's emotional response to a wrong should match its legal classification
The cultural norm that some wrongs deserve more attention than others
The requirement that the ongoing maintenance of a grievance should remain proportionate to the purpose it still serves
The psychological tendency to over-remember negative experiences
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay argues that continued rehearsal of an injury 'incurs costs that are no longer proportionate to the end' — the response must remain in proportion to the purpose it serves.
mcQuestions.hint: Look at the fifth paragraph for how the essay uses proportionality.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does the essay say the 'moral compulsion to remember' can do independently?
options: It can strengthen relationships by keeping shared histories alive
It can produce unreliable memories that distort the original wrong
It can motivate people to seek legal redress long after wrongs occurred
It can operate independently of whether remembering is actually serving any purpose beyond maintaining the grievance
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay warns: 'the moral compulsion to remember can operate independently of whether remembering is actually serving any purpose beyond the maintenance of the grievance itself.'

mcQuestions.prompt: What does the essay's final paragraph identify as not 'axioms'?
options: The claims that memory is always a virtue, that there is no grievance past its season, and that past ethics always trump present ethics
The claim that memory is more reliable than we think
The claim that forgetting is ever morally permissible
The claim that relationships can survive serious wrongs
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay ends by refusing to treat as axioms the ideas that 'memory is always and everywhere a virtue', that there is no grievance past its season, and 'that the ethics of the past is always more important than the ethics of the present.'

criticalThinkingQuestion: The essay argues that forgetting, under certain conditions, is an ethical act — not evasion but recalibration, a recognition that a wrong no longer demands the same response it once did. But there is an asymmetry worth examining: the person who caused the harm may be more motivated to recalibrate than the person who was harmed. How should an ethics of forgetting account for this asymmetry? Is 'readiness to forgive' a quality we should expect equally of both parties?
